Common Names: Mundane (English), Australian ash, Indian ash, Kenya coffeeshade (English), Mandania, Mndanii (Sino-Tibetan (Other)), Pink Cedar (English), Shingle Tree (English)
Synonyms: Acrocarpus fraxinifolius var. guangxiensis S.L.Mo & Y.Wei; Acrocarpus grandis (Miq.) Miq.; Acrocarpus combretiflorus Teijsm. & Binn.; Mezoneurum grande Miq.

blocks.text: Acrocarpus fraxinifolius is a widespread species distributed throughout tropical Asia and Africa and Central America, where its natural and biological distribution covers southeastern Asia. This species is widely planted both within and outside its natural distribution and has become naturalized in several countries. The population information is limited for this species, but the current population trend is considered stable as it occurs . Its extent of occurrence of 840,2194 km2 exceeds the threshold values for threatened categories. The species has been recorded from both protected areas and unprotected areas within its native distribution. It is therefore suspected that the number of locations for the species comfortably exceeds the threshold values for threatened categories. Moreover, the species is not known to be heavily exploited. The species is therefore assessed as Least Concern. opulation status and trends, as well as severity of threats to the species, would be beneficial to validate this conservation status.
blocks.text_rich: Acrocarpus fraxinifolius is a widespread species distributed throughout tropical Asia and Africa and Central America, where its natural and biological distribution covers southeastern Asia. This species is widely planted both within and outside its natural distribution and has become naturalized in several countries. The population information is limited for this species, but the current population trend is considered stable as it occurs . Its extent of occurrence of 840,2194 km<sup>2</sup> exceeds the threshold values for threatened categories. The species has been recorded from both protected areas and unprotected areas within its native distribution. It is therefore suspected that the number of locations for the species comfortably exceeds the threshold values for threatened categories. Moreover, the species is not known to be heavily exploited. The species is therefore assessed as Least Concern. opulation status and trends, as well as severity of threats to the species, would be beneficial to validate this conservation status.

blocks.text: This is a widespread species, considered to be native to tropical Asia where its natural and biological distribution covers India, Nepal, Bhutan, China, Myanmar, Vietnam, Bangladesh and Thailand. It is also considered native to western Indonesia, where it has been recorded from Sumatra and Java (Hou 1994). This species is widely planted both within and outside its natural distribution and has become naturalized in several countries in Africa and Central America, Tanzania, Uganda, Ethiopia and Kenya (Lemmens 2010 Witt and Luke 2017).
blocks.text_rich: This is a widespread species, considered to be native to tropical Asia where its natural and biological distribution covers India, Nepal, Bhutan, China, Myanmar, Vietnam, Bangladesh and Thailand. It is also considered native to western Indonesia, where it has been recorded from Sumatra and Java (Hou 1994). This species is widely planted both within and outside its natural distribution and has become naturalized in several countries in Africa and Central America, Tanzania, Uganda, Ethiopia and Kenya (Lemmens 2010 Witt and Luke 2017).

Please state reason for map not available:

Point data generated from herbarium specimen records and species observations (Sunarno and Ohashi 1997, GBIF.org 2021). While the point data does not include all collections or observations, it is representative of the geographic range as described by Adema et al. (2016) and the Plants of the World Online (2019) database.

blocks.text: This species has a widespread distribution throughout tropical Asia and naturalized in several countries, thus it is suspected that the global population is large the population trend is stable. Further research is recommended to better evaluate the population status and trend for this species.

blocks.text: Acrocarpus fraxinifolius  in tropical evergreen and sub-evergreen forest. In India it the evergreen forests hill slopes (up to 1200 m) of Western Ghats (Troup 1921 Rai 1976), tropical semi-evergreen forests of the eastern Himalayas and  the dominant tree species (Puri 1960). Annually it grows up to 1.3-3 m and can reach to a height of 50-60 m with a diameter 0.90-2.40 meters (Whitemore and Otarola 1976).  The species also cultivated in the high rainfall zone of Himachal Pradesh between 600-1200 m (Ghildyal 1989).  occurs  high rainfall, relatively cool highland areas (Laurie 1974) and flourish at an altitude ranging from 0-2000 m, precipitation from 500-3000 mm and average temperatures 14-26 °C (Martinez et al 2006). It is sensitive to drought and frost adapts to wide variation in rainfall (1900-5000 mm). Young leaves are characterized by their bright red appearance. Flowering of trees occur after the shedding of leaves. The bark surface is rough, pale grey and brown in colour. grows best in deep, well-drained, clay-loam soils with a pH of 4-7 and also thrives in acid soils and limestone areas (Cheng 1985).

The bark surface is rough, pale grey and brown in colour. grows best in deep, well-drained, clay-loam soils with a pH of 4-7 and also thrives in acid soils and limestone areas (Cheng 1985).

blocks.text: Acrocarpus fraxinifolius is one of the fast-growing indigenous tree species considered one of the mainstay of agro-forestry in Asian tropics cultivated as shade tree for tea and coffee plantation and also as a multipurpose avenue plantation (Lorenzi et al. 2003 Mishra et al. 2015). The sapwood of Acrocarpus is white and the heartwood is pinkish, which is commonly used in making veneers and plywood. It is also used as timber in furniture, door, windows, beams, rafters, etc. The wood is used as pulp for making paper (Orwa et al. 2009). The pulp is suitable for Kraft papereaves are suitable for mulching. imber is odorless and is suitable for fruit packing cases (Ghildyal 1989).
blocks.text_rich: Acrocarpus fraxinifolius is one of the fast-growing indigenous tree species considered one of the mainstay of agro-forestry in Asian tropics cultivated as shade tree for tea and coffee plantation and also as a multipurpose avenue plantation (Lorenzi et al. 2003 Mishra et al. 2015). The sapwood of Acrocarpus is white and the heartwood is pinkish, which is commonly used in making veneers and plywood. It is also used as timber in furniture, door, windows, beams, rafters, etc. The wood is used as pulp for making paper (Orwa et al. 2009). The pulp is suitable for Kraft papereaves are suitable for mulching. imber is odorless and is suitable for fruit packing cases (Ghildyal 1989).

blocks.text: The wood is not very durable and is prone to attack by fungi and insects, but it impregnates well. It is heavy, moderately hard, and compact; specific gravity varies between 0.55 and 0.7 g/cubic cm (Mishra et al., 2015). However, direct threats to this species known to be . It is therefore considered unlikely that any ongoing threats will affect the extinction risk of this species in future in the wild.

comments.anchor_context: Acrocarpus fraxinifolius  in tropical evergreen and sub-evergreen forest. In India it the evergreen forests hill slopes (up to 1200 m) of Western Ghats (Troup 1921 Rai 1976), tropical semi-evergreen forests of the eastern Himalayas and  the dominant tree species (Puri 1960). Annually it grows up to 1.3-3 m and can reach to a height of 50-60 m with a diameter 0.90-2.40 meters (Whitemore and Otarola 1976).  The species also cultivated in the high rainfall zone of Himachal Pradesh between 600-1200 m (Ghildyal 1989).  occurs  high rainfall, relatively cool highland areas (Laurie 1974) and flourish at an altitude ranging from 0-2000 m, precipitation from 500-3000 mm and average temperatures 14-26 °C (Martinez et al 2006). It is sensitive to drought and frost adapts to wide variation in rainfall (1900-5000 mm). Young leaves are characterized by their bright red appearance. Flowering of trees occur after the shedding of leaves. The bark surface is rough, pale grey and brown in colour. grows best in deep, well-drained, clay-loam soils with a pH of 4-7 and also thrives in acid soils and limestone areas (Cheng 1985). Habitat Season Suitability Major Importance? 1.5. Forest -> Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry

comments.anchor_text: The wood is not very durable and is prone to attack by fungi and insects, but it impregnates well.
comments.anchor_text_rich: The wood is not very durable and is prone to attack by fungi and insects, but it impregnates well.
comments.anchor_context: The wood is not very durable and is prone to attack by fungi and insects, but it impregnates well. It is heavy, moderately hard, and compact; specific gravity varies between 0.55 and 0.7 g/cubic cm (Mishra et al., 2015). However, direct threats to this species known to be . It is therefore considered unlikely that any ongoing threats will affect the extinction risk of this species in future in the wild.
